data.title: IonQ Announces Departure of Chief Revenue Officer
data.description:
On November 17, 2025, IonQ, Inc. announced that Rima Alameddine will step down as Chief Revenue Officer, effective November 24, 2025. Ms. Alameddine will receive severance benefits and continue in an advisory role until December 31, 2025.

IndividualRima Alameddine
RoleChief Revenue Officer
Type of ChangeDeparture
Effective DateNovember 24, 2025
ReasonNot disclosed
Replacement InfoNot disclosed
Background DetailsN/A
Board/Committee Role ChangesNot disclosed

Original SEC Filing: IonQ, Inc. [ IONQ ] - 8-K - Nov. 21, 2025

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Ethereum Lost 3.99% to $2763.43 — Data Talk
data.description:
Ethereum is down $114.95 today or 3.99% to $2763.43

  • Lowest 5 p.m. level since July 9, 2025 when it traded at $2740.75
  • Largest percentage decrease since Nov. 13, 2025, when it dropped 7.09%
  • Down eight of the past nine days
  • Down three consecutive days, down 10.77% over this period
  • Worst three day stretch since the three days ending Nov. 5, 2025, when it fell 10.84%
  • Down 12.71% this week
  • Worst weekly performance since the week ending Oct. 10, 2025, when it fell 14.41%
  • Down 28.4% month-to-date
  • Down 17.38% year-to-date
  • Down 44.23% from its all-time intraday high of $4955.23 on Aug. 24, 2025
  • Down 16.76% from 52 weeks ago (Nov. 22, 2024), when it traded at $3319.92
  • Up 88.95% from its 52-week intraday low of $1387.85 on April 9, 2025
  • Traded as low as $2622.35; lowest intraday level since July 9, 2025 when it hit $2592.19
  • Down 8.89% at today's intraday low; Largest intraday % decrease since Nov. 4, 2025, when it was down as much as 14.51%

Note: The Ethereum price is a 5 p.m. ET snapshot from Kraken

Data compiled by Dow Jones Market Data

data.title: Fitch to Rate Utility Debt Securitization Auth Restructuring Bonds Ser 2025; Presale Issued
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-New York-21 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings expects to rate the $1.03 billion utility tariff restructuring bonds series 2025TE-1 (Green Bonds) and 2025TE-2, to be issued by the Utility Debt Securitization Authority (UDSA) restructuring bonds, series 2025 'AAA(EXP)sf'. The Rating Outlook is Stable.

The collateral for the restructuring bonds primarily consists of the restructuring property, which represents the right to impose, charge and collect through the applicable non-bypassable restructuring charges (RCs) payable by retail electric customers.

Utility Debt Securitization Authority Restructuring Bonds, Series 2025

----2025TE-1 T-1 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-1 T-2 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-1 T-3 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-1 T-4 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-1 T-5 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-1 T-6 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-1 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-10 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-11 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-12 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-13 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-14 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-15 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-16 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-17 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-18 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-19 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-2 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-20 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-21 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-22 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-23 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-24 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-3 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-4 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-5 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-6 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-7 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-8 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----2025TE-2 T-9 ; Long Term Rating; Expected Rating; AAA(EXP)sf; Rating Outlook Stable

Transaction Summary

The series 2025 bonds are expected to be issued in two series: 2025TE-1 and 2025TE-2, totaling $112 million and $914 million, respectively. This issuance is the eighth series by UDSA. UDSA is a special-purpose corporate municipal instrumentality, a body corporate and politic, a political subdivision and a public benefit corporation of the State of New York.

The transaction is expected to be issued in 30 tranches. The bonds' scheduled maturity is not anticipated to exceed 20 years, with the legal final maturity about two years after the expected maturity. The bonds will be issued under Part B of Chapter 173, Laws of New York ("LIPA Reform Act"), signed by the Governor on July 29, 2013 and effective Aug. 28, 2013, and Financing Order No. 8, approved by the New York Public Authorities Control Board on May 18, 2022 and irrevocable on June 17, 2022.

The securitization law sets a process through which the issuer and the Long Island Power Authority (the "Authority") may refinance existing debt through a financing order, which authorizes the creation of restructuring property and the issuance of restructuring bonds. The 2025 restructuring bonds are secured by, and payable from, the restructuring property created by Financing Order No. 8. The bonds will be issued to purchase, redeem, pay or defease debt of UDSA, to finance system-resiliency costs of the Authority, and pay upfront financing costs.

The RC will be allocated across all of LIPA's customer classes based on their actual electricity consumption. As a result, the transaction cash flows will depend on the variance in overall energy consumption. The initial aggregate RC charge, post issuance of the series 2025 bonds, is expected to represent approximately $16.78 per customer, or 9.27% of the average residential customer bill.

KEY RATING DRIVERS

Legal Risk and Regulatory Framework - Consistent: The strength and stability of the underlying RC are established by the financing order approved by the New York Public Authorities Control Board in May 2022. The financing order establishes the irrevocable, binding and non-bypassable RC, and defines bondholders' property rights in the securitized utility tariff property. The financing order contains the key legal elements required by Fitch's "U.S. Utility Tariff/Stranded Cost Bonds Rating Criteria" for a utility tariff securitization.

Credit Analysis (Revenue Stability) - Positive: Cash flow supporting utility tariff bonds comes from RC payments by all current and future retail electric customers in the utility's service territory. Changes in the customer base, particularly customer count, may result from new technologies, the overall economy, natural disasters, demographic shifts, or usage changes. These shifts create risk in this asset class given the bonds' longer tenor, but the true-up mechanism mitigates it.

Originator Utility Operations - Positive: The originator, LIPA, operates a strong utility, as evidenced by its 'A+'/Stable Issuer Default Rating (IDR). The IDR was upgraded from 'A/Positive' to the current rating in July 2024, and was affirmed in August 2025, driven by improving leverage ratios, cash flow and coverage metrics. Fitch expects these metrics to remain stable under stressed scenarios.

Structural and Credit Enhancement Analysis - Positive: Credit enhancement is primarily provided by a true-up mechanism. Mandatory annual true-up filings adjust the RC to ensure collections are sufficient to provide all scheduled payments of principal and interest, pay fees and expenses and replenish the debt service reserve subaccount to at least 0.50% of the outstanding principal amount and operating reserve subaccount to 0.50% of the initial issuance amount over the following 12 months. Interim optional true-ups may occur at any time, if necessary, but must meet certain defined parameters.

Cash Flow Analysis - Neutral: For this transaction, Fitch reviewed cash flows provided by transaction parties, as the initial tariff rate is below 10%, in line with Fitch's "U.S. Utility Tariff/Stranded Cost Bonds Rating Criteria," dated November 2023. Fitch's 'AAAsf' scenario stresses key model variables, including electricity consumption in each customer class, chargeoff rates and delinquencies. Fitch assesses that special tariffs (under all scenarios) over 20% of the average residential customer bill across a long financing term would generally be inconsistent with a 'AAAsf' rating.

Under Fitch's 'AAAsf' stress, the peak aggregate RC for all outstanding series sponsored by the authority exceeds the 20% thresholds with a peak of 22.8%. However, Fitch views the breach as commensurate with a 'AAAsf' rating because it occurs only in two early periods, lasts one period each time, and only under 'AAAsf' stresses.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

Forecast variances requiring larger than expected true-ups, which increases the aggregate RC as a percentage of the average residential bill over 20% for longer financing periods could put downward rating pressure on the transaction.

In September 2025, Fitch revised its "Global Economic Outlook", raising its world growth forecasts for 2025 moderately on better-than-expected incoming data for 2Q25. Evidence now points to an underlying U.S. slowdown in 'hard' economic data and eurozone growth surprises partly reflect U.S. tariff front-running. Fitch expects world GDP to slow significantly this year. Fitch does not expect an impact on the transaction.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

Given the bonds are rated 'AAAsf', Fitch did not consider upside stresses.

CRITERIA VARIATION

Using its "U.S. Utility Tariff/Stranded Cost Bonds Rating Criteria," Fitch sets the 'AAAsf' rating case forecast variance at 5.0x the historical five- to 10-year peak absolute forecast variance. For this transaction, Fitch used a base-case absolute forecast variance of 4.28%, lower than the variances observed during 2020-2022, a variation from Fitch's "U.S. Utility Tariff/Stranded Cost Bonds Rating Criteria". Fitch treated the 2020-2022 as less relevant for this analysis because the pandemic materially altered consumer behavior (more time at home), producing unusually large forecast errors. Without this criteria variation, the bonds could not have achieved a 'AAAsf' rating.

USE OF THIRD PARTY DUE DILIGENCE PURSUANT TO SEC RULE 17G -10

Form ABS Due Diligence-15E was not provided to, or reviewed by, Fitch in relation to this rating action.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. REPRESENTATIONS, WARRANTIES AND ENFORCEMENT MECHANISMS A description of the transaction's representations, warranties and enforcement mechanisms (RW&Es) that are disclosed in the offering document and which relate to the underlying asset pool is available by clicking the link to the Appendix. The appendix also contains a comparison of these RW&Es to those Fitch considers typical for the asset class as detailed in the Special Report titled 'Representations, Warranties and Enforcement Mechanisms in Global Structured Finance Transactions'.

A description of the transaction's representations, warranties and enforcement mechanisms (RW&Es) that are disclosed in the offering document and which relate to the underlying asset pool is available by clicking the link to the Appendix. The appendix also contains a comparison of these RW&Es to those Fitch considers typical for the asset class as detailed in the Special Report titled 'Representations, Warranties and Enforcement Mechanisms in Global Structured Finance Transactions'.

A description of the transaction's representations, warranties and enforcement mechanisms (RW&Es) that are disclosed in the offering document and which relate to the underlying asset pool is available by clicking the link to the Appendix. The appendix also contains a comparison of these RW&Es to those Fitch considers typical for the asset class as detailed in its report "Representations, Warranties and Enforcement Mechanisms in Global Structured Finance Transactions".

ESG Considerations

Unless otherwise disclosed in this section, the highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3'. This means ESG issues are credit-neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visit www.fitchratings.com/esg

Additional information is available on www.fitchratings.com

data.title: Amex Announces Grant of Stock Options and Restricted Share Units
data.description:
Montreal, Quebec--(Newsfile Corp. - November 21, 2025) - Amex Exploration Inc.  (FSE: MX0) (OTCQX: AMXEF) ("Amex" or the "Company") announces a grant of 3,250,000 incentive stock options (the "Options") and 2,026,000 restricted share units (the "RSUs") to certain directors, officers, employees, and consultants to the Company.

Each Option is exercisable to acquire one common share of the Company (a "Share") at a price of $2.85 per Share, for a period of five years from the date of grant. The RSUs shall vest in three equal tranches, with one-third vesting on each of the first, second, and third anniversaries of the date of grant. Upon vesting, each RSU shall entitle the holder to receive one Share. All grants of Options and RSUs are subject to the Company's omnibus equity incentive plan (the "Equity Incentive Plan"), which was approved by shareholders at the Company's annual general and special meeting of shareholders held on June 30, 2025 (the "Meeting"). A copy of the Equity Incentive Plan is included in the Company's management information circular in respect of the Meeting dated May 12, 2025 available via the Company's profile on SEDAR+ at www.sedarplus.ca.

All of the Options and RSUs (and any Shares issuable upon exercise or settlement thereof) will be subject to a four month and one day hold period from the date of grant pursuant to the policies of the TSX Venture Exchange).

About Amex

Amex Exploration Inc. has made significant high-grade gold discoveries, along with copper-rich volcanogenic massive sulphide (VMS) zones, at its 100%-owned Perron Gold Project, located approximately 110 kilometres north of Rouyn-Noranda, Quebec. The Project comprises 117 contiguous claims (45.18 km²) and hosts both bulk-tonnage and high-grade gold mineralization styles.

When combined with the adjacent Perron West Project, which includes 48 claims (17.37 km²) in Quebec and 35 claims (134.55 km²) in Ontario, the consolidated land package spans a district-scale 197.52 km². This extensive property lies within highly prospective geology favourable for both high-grade gold and VMS mineralization.

The Project benefits from excellent infrastructure: it is accessible by a year-round road, located just 20 minutes from an airport, and approximately 8 km from the Town of Normétal. It is also in close proximity to several process plants owned by major gold producers.

For further information please contact:

Victor Cantore

President and Chief Executive Officer

victor.cantore@amexexploration.com

Amex Exploration: +1-514-866-8209

Neither the TSX Venture Exchange nor its Regulation Services Provider (as that term is defined in the policies of the TSX Venture Exchange) accepts responsibility for the adequacy or accuracy of this release.

To view the source version of this press release, please visit https://www.newsfilecorp.com/release/275565

data.title: Bitcoin Just Had Its Worst Week in Months. Why Cryptos and Stocks Are Going Different Directions Today. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Anita Hamilton and George Glover

Cryptocurrencies missed out on the broader market rally Friday. While the tech-heavy Nasdaq shook off its Thursday slump, crypto investors aren't ready to take on more risk.

Bitcoin was trading down nearly 2% at $84,859 Friday, while all three major stock indexes were up by about the same amount.

The simple explanation is that Bitcoin has dropped more than 30% since its Oct. 6 peak, leaving many holders underwater on some of their purchases. Because crypto is often held in margin accounts, some likely had to sell to cover their losses, pushing the price down even further.

The broader stock market, while volatile, is still riding relatively high. The S&P 500 is down just 4% from it's late October peak of around 6,890.

There could be more pain ahead for Bitcoin. "From a demand perspective it appears there is an early, yet growing, sense of concern that could evolve into full-on panic if the selling pressure continues to intensify further than it already has, as lower prices would prompt more selling in a doom loop of sorts," analysts at the financial research firm Sevens Report wrote on Friday.

The world's largest crypto is trading 33% below its record high of $126,272 in early October. It's also on pace for its worst week since November 2022, when the collapse of crypto exchange FTX sparked a brutal selloff.

Other digital assets weren't doing any better. Ethereum dropped 3.8%, Solana fell 3.9%, and XRP tumbled 2.8%.

Several factors have driven the recent plunge in crypto prices.

First, investors are unsure if the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates next month. If the central bank holds borrowing costs steady, that would make Bitcoin and its peers less appealing relative to interest-bearing investments such as bonds and savings accounts.

Second, the market has decided this is a good time to rotate out of risk assets, amid questions about lofty artificial-intelligence valuations.

A third issue is that many investors bought Bitcoin at around $90,000. With it now trading below that, they may be hesitant to keep buying while their investments are underwater, especially if they borrowed money to buy it and are now facing margin calls. That is when brokers demand more cash from investors to cover the loans. That, in turn, can lead to forced selling, which puts additional negative pressure on the asset's price.

Don't count on the crash stopping just here. There could be sizable price swings next week, given that volumes are likely to be thin from Thursday onward due to Thanksgiving. That means it wouldn't take much pressure for Bitcoin to continue dropping.

"Liquidity will likely dry up across markets this week and heading into the holiday season," Adam Morgan McCarthy, head of research at the crypto data provider Kaiko, told Barron's. "This could exacerbate moves if investors continue to par positions before the holidays and liquidity dries up more."

Write to Anita Hamilton at anita.hamilton@barrons.com and George Glover at george.glover@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Moody's Ratings Upgrades Italy on Expectation of Declining Debt
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

Moody's boosted Italy's sovereign-credit rating on expectations for a decline in government debt.

The ratings agency on Friday upgraded Italy's rating one level to Baa2 from Baa3. The outlook was revised to stable from positive.

Moody's said it expects Italy's government-debt burden will decline from 2027 onward due to economic and fiscal reforms under its National Recovery and Resilience Plan, as well as the possibility of further policy actions supporting growth beyond the plan's deadline in August 2026.

Italy's government appears committed to maintaining high public investment beyond the end of the plan, which should boost the country's credit profile through improved infrastructure and competitiveness and bolster economic growth, Moody's said.

Italy's robust banking sector and strong private-sector balance sheets should also support economic stability, the ratings agency said.

Moody's projects Italy's debt will reach slightly above 130% of GDP by 2034 from its estimate of about 137% for 2025.

Italy's stable outlook, meanwhile, takes into account its credit strengths, including reforms to boost public-sector efficiency and the broader business environment that could lead to more substantial improvement in its growth outlook, Moody's said.

The outlook also factors in its challenges, the ratings agency said, noting that a decline in Italy's high debt burden is reliant on robust gross domestic product growth and rising primary surpluses.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Moody's grants Italy its first rating upgrade in 23 years
data.description:
Global ratings agency Moody's on Friday upgraded Italy's sovereign rating to "Baa2" from "Baa3", citing the country's consistent track record of political and policy stability.

"Italy is making good progress in meeting the milestones and targets of the National Recovery and Resilience Plan, leading among all EU countries in terms of number of payment requests and disbursements," the agency said in a statement.

Moody's has upgraded Italy's rating for the first time since May 2002, when it passed from "Aa3" to "Aa2", and the rating has not changed since a downgrade in October 2018.

"We are pleased with Moody's upgrade, the first in 23 years. This is a further confirmation of the regained confidence in this government and, therefore, in Italy," Giancarlo Giorgetti, the country's economy minister, said in a statement.

Italy expects its 2025 deficit to fall below 3% of GDP this year, ahead of schedule. The government had earlier pledged to cut the fiscal deficit to 3.3% of GDP this year, down from 3.4% in 2024.

Reducing the fiscal deficit to less than 3% of GDP this year would allow Italy to exit the EU infringement procedure for excessive deficits by mid-2026.

"We expect that Italy's high government debt burden will gradually decline from 2027 onwards," Moody's added.

Moody's further revised Italy's outlook to "stable" from "positive", citing the balance between its credit strengths and challenges. The agency had lifted Italy's outlook in May to positive, on stronger-than-expected fiscal performance and a stable political backdrop.

While challenges from Italy's aging population and high debt burden persist, fiscal discipline and political steadiness provide some relief.

data.title: Captivision CEO Garrabrant Named Interim CFO
data.description:
By Terell Wright

Captivision Chief Executive Officer Gary Garrabrant will become the company's chief financial officer on an interim basis effective immediately.

Garrabrant succeeds Anthony Page and comes as the company moves to implement a strategic transformation, Captivision said Friday. Page will continue to support the company in a financial reporting capacity with respect to audit and other required filing obligations.

The digital display company in August said it is exploring strategic options for Captivision Korea, its wholly owned subsidiary, as it looks to optimize value for stakeholders.

Write to Terell Wright at terell.wright@wsj.com

data.title: Unum Group EVP and CFO Steven Andrew Zabel Sells 7,500 Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameZabel Steven Andrew
RelationshipEVP, Chief Financial Officer, 10% Owner
TypeSell
Amount$572,865
SEC FilingForm 4

Steven Andrew Zabel, EVP and Chief Financial Officer of Unum Group, and a 10% owner, sold 7,500 shares of Common Stock on November 19, 2025, at a weighted average price of $76.382 per share, totaling $572,865. Following the transaction, Zabel directly owns 113,807 shares of Unum Group, including 38,724 restricted stock units, 3,852 stock success units, and 71,231 shares of common stock.

SEC Filing: Unum Group [ UNM ] - Form 4 - Nov. 21, 2025
